content.heading: System Requirements
paragraphs: A URL shortener service creates a short alias for a long URL. When users click the short link, they are redirected to the original long URL. This is a classic system design interview question.
Functional Requirements: 1. Given a long URL, generate a shorter, unique alias. 2. When users access the short link, redirect them to the original link. 3. Links expire after a certain timespan.
Non-Functional Requirements: 1. Highly available. 2. URL redirection must happen in real-time with minimal latency. 3. Short links should not be predictable.

content.heading: Capacity Estimation
paragraphs: Assume 100 million new URLs generated per month. Read/Write ratio is typically 10:1 (more redirects than creations). Therefore, 1 billion redirections per month.
QPS (Queries Per Second): Writes = 100M / (30 * 24 * 3600) ≈ 40 URLs/sec. Reads = 400 URLs/sec.
Storage: Assuming we store URLs for 5 years and each URL mapping takes ~500 bytes. 100M * 12 * 5 = 6 Billion URLs. 6B * 500 bytes = 3 TB of storage.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: Clients talk to an API Gateway/Load Balancer. The LB routes requests to Web Servers.
When creating a short URL, the Web Server asks a Unique ID Generator for a new ID. The ID is converted to Base62 (a-z, A-Z, 0-9). The mapping (short_url -> long_url) is stored in a database.

For redirection, the Web Server looks up the short URL in a Cache (Redis) first. If a miss, it hits the Database, caches the result, and returns a HTTP 301 (Permanent Redirect) or 302 (Temporary Redirect).

points: Use Base62 encoding to convert a numeric ID to a short string (62 characters: A-Z, a-z, 0-9).
A 7-character Base62 string can support 62^7 ≈ 3.5 Trillion URLs.
A relational database (MySQL) or NoSQL (Cassandra) can be used. NoSQL scales writes better.
Pre-generate IDs using a Token Generation Service (like ZooKeeper) to avoid DB locks and collisions.
HTTP 301 (Permanent Redirect) vs 302 (Temporary). 301 allows browser caching (faster) but prevents tracking analytics on every click. 302 forces a server hit every time.

content.heading: System Requirements
paragraphs: Designing a chat system like WhatsApp or Messenger involves handling high volume, low latency messaging with status indicators.
Functional Requirements: 1. 1-on-1 chat. 2. Group chat (max 100 members). 3. Online/Offline presence indicator. 4. Message history across multiple devices.
Non-Functional Requirements: 1. Real-time delivery (low latency). 2. High availability. 3. Eventual consistency for message history.

content.heading: High Level Architecture
paragraphs: A chat system cannot rely purely on standard HTTP because HTTP is client-initiated. The server needs to push messages to clients. Therefore, persistent connections using WebSockets are standard.
The system is split into two parts: Stateless services (login, user profile, signup) and Stateful services (the chat servers managing active WebSocket connections).

content.heading: Message Routing and Storage
paragraphs: When Alice sends a message to Bob: Alice sends the message to her connected Chat Server. The Chat Server assigns a message ID, persists it to a database (like Cassandra or HBase, which handle high write throughput well), and publishes it to a Message Queue.
A routing service looks up which Chat Server Bob is currently connected to (using a distributed cache like Redis). It routes the message to Bob's Chat Server, which pushes it to Bob's device.
If Bob is offline, the message remains in the database. When Bob comes online, his device fetches unread messages via an API call.

points: WebSockets are preferred for real-time bidirectional communication over long-polling.
A NoSQL Key-Value store (Cassandra, HBase) is ideal for chat history due to the massive write volume.
Message ordering can be maintained using a local sequence number generator per chat thread.
Presence service (online/offline status) works via heartbeat signals from clients every few seconds. If heartbeats stop, the user is marked offline.
